content.text: Integration testing verifies that independently developed components work correctly when connected together. Where unit tests isolate a single function or class, integration tests exercise real interactions between modules, databases, message queues, and external services — confirming that the contracts between components hold in practice, not just in theory.

An integration test exercises two or more real components together. The word "real" is key: in contrast to unit tests that use mocks for every external boundary, integration tests instantiate actual collaborators — a real database connection, a real HTTP client, a real file system — and exercise the system through them.

The scope of "integration" is a spectrum. Narrow integration tests wire two modules together (e.g., a repository class and a SQLite database). Broad integration tests exercise an entire request path through an application (e.g., an HTTP request through a FastAPI app, through business logic, into a Postgres database, and back). Both are integration tests; the difference is the number of boundaries crossed.

Integration testing sits above unit tests and below end-to-end (E2E) tests in the classic test pyramid. It provides a medium-granularity safety net that catches an important class of bugs that unit tests cannot: integration failures, where each component works correctly in isolation but fails when connected to its real collaborators.
Unit tests catch logic bugs. Integration tests catch contract bugs — mismatches between what one component expects and what another provides. Common integration failures include:
A repository layer that constructs SQL with a column name that was renamed in the schema.
An HTTP client that sends a field in the wrong format (string vs. integer) that the mock silently accepted but the real API rejects.
A queue consumer that handles message deserialization differently than the producer serializes it.
ORM model configuration that generates N+1 queries instead of the expected single query.
Configuration that works in development but fails in CI because of a missing environment variable.
These failures are invisible to unit tests precisely because the mock duplicates the assumption that is wrong. Integration tests break that assumption by using real components.

For agentic AI systems, integration testing is especially critical because the pipeline often spans LLM calls, tool execution, database reads/writes, and external API calls. Even if each step is unit-tested, the pipeline as a whole can fail due to schema mismatches, missing fields in tool outputs, or unexpected LLM response formats. Integration tests at the pipeline level catch these before they surface in production.
Think of integration testing as checking whether the

cables connect correctly after verifying each component with a bench test. A unit test is a bench test: does this single component behave according to its spec when inputs are controlled? An integration test plugs the components into each other and powers the system on: do the signals flow correctly end-to-end?

A useful analogy from plumbing: a pressure test for a single valve (unit test) does not tell you whether the joints between pipes leak (integration). You need to pressurize the entire run of pipe to find joint failures.
— integration tests are the only automated test that can detect mismatches between a component's expected interface and what its collaborator actually provides.
— environment variables, connection strings, and infrastructure configuration are verified by integration tests; unit tests with mocks never exercise them.
— query generation, cascade behaviour, and transaction semantics are exercised against a real database engine.
— JSON encoding/decoding, protobuf mismatches, and date format discrepancies only appear when both sides of the boundary run together.
— a green integration suite means the application can connect to its dependencies and process requests, not just that isolated logic is correct.
— integration tests document the exact expected interaction between components at a level that is closer to the system's real behaviour than unit tests.
— database connections, HTTP calls, and process startup add latency. A suite of 100 integration tests may take 30–120 seconds; the same number of unit tests might take under one second.
— integration tests require running real infrastructure (databases, queues, external services). Docker Compose or test containers ease this, but add CI complexity.
— when an integration test fails, the bug could be in any of the components involved. Debugging requires navigating multiple layers.
— tests that share a real database must carefully manage state: each test must start from a known state and clean up after itself. Failing to do so causes flaky, order-dependent tests.
— renaming a database table, changing an API response schema, or upgrading a dependency can break integration tests more broadly than unit tests.

The most reliable pattern for database-backed integration tests is to wrap each test in a database transaction and roll back at the end. This ensures every test starts from a clean state without the cost of recreating the schema.
fixture to wrap each test in a transaction. Django's
class provides this automatically. For applications without ORM support, a simpler approach is to truncate affected tables in a teardown fixture.

Testcontainers spins up Docker containers on demand during test setup and tears them down afterward. This eliminates the need for a shared development database, ensures a clean state, and makes CI straightforward. The
library provides Postgres, Redis, RabbitMQ, and many other containers.

FastAPI and Flask both ship test clients that drive the application's routing layer without a real network socket. These are ideal for integration tests of API endpoints because they exercise the full request path (routing, validation, business logic, database) while remaining in-process and fast.
The diagram below shows where integration tests sit in the test scope pyramid and which boundaries they exercise.

Integration tests occupy the middle layer: they use real components (database, queue, file system) but stop short of exercising the full system through a browser or external network call. This position lets them catch boundary failures that unit tests miss while remaining fast enough to run in CI on every push.
Integration tests are most valuable for:
Repository or data access layer — every query, join, and update should be tested against the real database engine.
API endpoints — exercise the full request path: routing, serialization, validation, business logic, and persistence.
Message consumers — verify that a queue consumer correctly parses and processes messages serialized by the producer.
External service adapters — test the adapter against a real or sandboxed version of the external service.
Authentication and authorization — verify that middleware correctly enforces access control on real requests.
Background jobs and scheduled tasks — confirm that a job reads and writes data correctly to a real store.

Do not use integration tests as a substitute for unit tests. Logic that can be tested in isolation (parsing, calculations, state machines) is better covered by fast unit tests. Integration tests should focus on the boundaries.
The following example demonstrates integration testing a FastAPI endpoint that reads from a PostgreSQL database using SQLAlchemy.

# conftest.py
import pytest
from sqlalchemy import create_engine
from sqlalchemy.orm import sessionmaker
from fastapi.testclient import TestClient
from myapp.main import app
from myapp.db import Base, get_session

# Use a separate test database URL (or testcontainers)
TEST_DB_URL = "postgresql://user:pass@localhost:5432/testdb"

@pytest.fixture(scope="session")
def engine():
    eng = create_engine(TEST_DB_URL)
    Base.metadata.create_all(eng)
    yield eng
    Base.metadata.drop_all(eng)

@pytest.fixture()
def db_session(engine):
    """Each test gets a transaction that is rolled back on teardown."""
    connection = engine.connect()
    transaction = connection.begin()
    Session = sessionmaker(bind=connection)
    session = Session()
    yield session
    session.close()
    transaction.rollback()
    connection.close()

@pytest.fixture()
def client(db_session):
    app.dependency_overrides[get_session] = lambda: db_session
    with TestClient(app) as c:
        yield c
    app.dependency_overrides.clear()

# tests/test_items_api.py
import pytest
from myapp.models import Item

def test_create_item(client, db_session):
    response = client.post("/items", json={"name": "Widget", "price": 9.99})
    assert response.status_code == 201
    data = response.json()
    assert data["name"] == "Widget"
    assert data["price"] == 9.99
    assert "id" in data
    # Confirm it was persisted
    item = db_session.get(Item, data["id"])
    assert item is not None
    assert item.name == "Widget"

def test_list_items_empty(client):
    response = client.get("/items")
    assert response.status_code == 200
    assert response.json() == []

def test_get_nonexistent_item(client):
    response = client.get("/items/99999")
    assert response.status_code == 404
    assert response.json()["detail"] == "Item not found"
Step 3: Use testcontainers for CI portability

# conftest.py (testcontainers version)
import pytest
from testcontainers.postgres import PostgresContainer
from sqlalchemy import create_engine
from myapp.db import Base

@pytest.fixture(scope="session")
def postgres_container():
    with PostgresContainer("postgres:15") as pg:
        yield pg

@pytest.fixture(scope="session")
def engine(postgres_container):
    url = postgres_container.get_connection_url()
    eng = create_engine(url)
    Base.metadata.create_all(eng)
    yield eng
    Base.metadata.drop_all(eng)

# tests/test_consumer.py
import pytest
import json
from myapp.consumer import process_order_message
from myapp.models import Order

def test_process_valid_order_message(db_session):
    message = json.dumps({
        "order_id": "ORD-001",
        "customer_id": "CUST-42",
        "items": [{"sku": "A1", "qty": 2}],
        "total": 19.98
    })
    process_order_message(message, db_session)
    order = db_session.query(Order).filter_by(order_id="ORD-001").one()
    assert order.customer_id == "CUST-42"
    assert order.total == 19.98

def test_process_malformed_message_does_not_persist(db_session):
    # Missing required 'order_id' field
    message = json.dumps({"customer_id": "CUST-42"})
    with pytest.raises(ValueError):
        process_order_message(message, db_session)
    assert db_session.query(Order).count() == 0

A fraud detection pipeline at a payments company used integration tests to verify its decision engine against a real Redis cache and a real Postgres store. The pipeline read recent transaction history from Postgres, checked a Redis blocklist, ran a scoring function, and wrote the decision back to Postgres.

The team ran integration tests on every PR using Testcontainers. Each container started fresh, the test suite seeded known transaction histories, and asserted on the full decision output including the written record. When the team upgraded Redis from version 6 to 7, two integration tests immediately caught a changed return format in one command. The bug would have passed all unit tests (which mocked Redis entirely) and would have reached production.

The suite ran in 45 seconds in CI, which the team found acceptable for the confidence it provided. They kept unit tests covering the scoring logic (which ran in under 2 seconds) and reserved integration tests for the boundary layers.

Tests that do not clean up after themselves create hidden dependencies between test execution order. A test that passes in isolation may fail when run after another test that left stale data. Use transaction rollback or table truncation to guarantee a clean slate.

Integration tests are slower and harder to debug than unit tests. Complex business logic should be unit-tested first. Integration tests verify that the components connect correctly — they should not be the primary coverage mechanism for logic branches.
An integration test that mocks its database is a unit test in disguise. If the intent is to test the integration between the application and the database, the database must be real.
Slow test suites from unmanaged containers

Starting a new Docker container for every test rather than every session dramatically inflates test time. Scope fixtures at the session or module level and use transaction rollback to reset state cheaply between tests.
Scope the container fixture at the function level — starts a container for every test.
Scope the container fixture at the session level — one container for the entire run.
Separate integration tests from unit tests
). Run unit tests on every save; integration tests on every commit.
— eliminates environment drift between developer machines and CI, ensuring integration tests run against the same versions of dependencies.
— cheaper and more reliable than truncating tables or recreating the schema between tests.
— seed only the minimum data the test requires. Large shared fixtures make it hard to understand what each test depends on and why it fails.
— integration tests are the right place to test infrastructure error handling: database connection failure, network timeout, full disk. Use dependency injection to inject failing collaborators.
— after calling an endpoint or function, assert on the database state, not just the return value. An endpoint that returns 200 but fails to write to the database is broken regardless.
— pin Postgres, Redis, and other infrastructure versions in Testcontainers to prevent silent behaviour changes from version upgrades breaking the test suite.
Integration test suite performance degrades as the number of tests grows. Common patterns for managing performance:
— start infrastructure containers once per test session, not once per test. The overhead of container startup is amortized across all tests.
— pytest-xdist parallelises test execution across multiple workers. Each worker needs its own database schema or schema prefix to avoid conflicts.
— if tests in a module share a common data setup, scope the seed fixture at the module level so setup runs once for all tests in the module.
— large integration suites can be sharded across multiple CI jobs (e.g., GitHub Actions matrix) to reduce wall-clock time.

A well-organised integration test suite for a medium-sized service typically runs in 30–90 seconds. Suites that take longer than 5 minutes indicate either excessive scope (too many E2E concerns mixed in) or inadequate state management.
pytest is the standard Python integration test runner. Use
library provides ready-made containers for Postgres, MySQL, Redis, RabbitMQ, Kafka, MinIO, and others. Each container exposes a real connection URL that can be passed to the application under test.
Both frameworks provide in-process HTTP test clients that exercise the full application stack without a network socket. These are the standard tools for API integration tests in Python.
For async applications (FastAPI with async handlers, aiohttp),
provides async fixture and test function support, including async database sessions and async HTTP clients via
A test data factory library that simplifies creating and seeding database objects in integration tests. Pairs well with SQLAlchemy. Use
Integration tests should include security-relevant scenarios that exercise real middleware. A test that only uses
without authentication headers can mask an authentication middleware bug where credentials are never checked.

Test databases must not contain real production data. Use synthetic seed data only. CI pipelines should run integration tests in isolated environments where test databases cannot interfere with shared development or production databases.

Flaky integration tests are a reliability signal. A test that fails intermittently is usually caused by shared mutable state (a test that did not clean up), a race condition (an async operation that sometimes completes before the assertion), or non-deterministic ordering. Flaky tests should be fixed immediately, not quarantined and ignored, because they erode trust in the entire suite.
Integration tests verify that independently developed components work correctly when connected together — unit tests alone cannot detect contract mismatches.
Use real collaborators (database, queue, HTTP client), not mocks, to get integration tests' full benefit.
Wrap each test in a database transaction and roll back to guarantee a clean state cheaply.
Testcontainers eliminates environment drift by providing on-demand Docker containers for Postgres, Redis, Kafka, and others.
Scope container fixtures at the session level and reset state between tests via transactions, not container restarts.
Keep integration tests focused on boundaries (API endpoints, repositories, adapters); test business logic with faster unit tests.
Security and error-path integration tests (authentication, missing fields, infrastructure failures) are as important as happy-path tests.
